# Questions in Livestream 124

Live Stream #124: Questioning Book Learning

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=382" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:22</a> | Is Caleb's name effectively a derogatory dog nickname? | He says yes, though like many nicknames it could be repurposed rather than functioning as a simple insult in every context. | He answers that Caleb was a derogatory nickname, but compares it with other biblical nicknames that people later embraced as monikers or terms of endearment. His point is that the negative origin does not mean the name only functioned as an insult afterward. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=476" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:56</a> | How could people worship idols they made with their own hands? | He says ancient idol practice was about a divine presence inhabiting ritual images, not about confusing raw stone with deity. | He explains idolatry as making a proxy body for a god, angelic being, or demonic being to inhabit. In his description, worshipers were not revering mere stone; they were trying to bind, coax, bribe, feed, clothe, and keep the spiritual presence attached to the image so it would act for them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=666" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:06</a> | If Jephthah meant to sacrifice his daughter, could priests have stopped him? | He treats the scene as morally and ritually troubled, while noting the text does not give us a clean priestly intervention mechanism. | He says Jephthah was in a backwater region without priests, police, or a magistrate over him, and that the sacrifice would have been private ritual practice at his own altar. Levites might have warned him about a possible way out of a rash oath, but the text does not say they did or that Jephthah would have listened. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=820" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:40</a> | Why would anyone buy a donkey's head during the famine in 2 Kings 6:25? | He explains it as desperation food in an extreme siege context rather than a normal culinary choice. | He says the head would have been bought for food during famine: cheeks, tongue, brain, marrow, eyes, collagen, and the skull for stock or stew could all provide nutrition. It would not be normal kosher eating, but famine pushes people toward less desirable protein sources, making the price understandable in a siege. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=1097" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:17</a> | If uncovering Noah's nakedness means relations with Noah's wife, what are Shem and Japheth doing in Genesis 9:23? | He says they cover the shame without looking; "nakedness" works as a Torah euphemism. | He reads "nakedness" through the Torah's common euphemism for sexual relations with a relative, so the shame involves Noah's wife rather than ordinary male nudity. Shem and Japheth walk backward with a garment so they can cover her without looking, thereby covering the shame of what happened. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=1377" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:57</a> | Do scholars really think Genesis 1-11 was written during the exile, and if so what about the rest of Genesis? | He walks through the scholarly claim but does not present exile-only composition as a settled fact. | He acknowledges many scholars, even some conservative ones, argue for an exilic setting because of Mesopotamian parallels. He favors an earlier wilderness-era core later collated, arguing Genesis also reflects Egyptian vocabulary and mythic background and would have served as an "anti-myth" for a mixed group coming out of Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=1759" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:19</a> | Did the anointing oil in Exodus 30:23 contain cannabis? | He says probably not; Targum Onkelos points instead toward sweet calamus or sweet flag. | He says the cannabis proposal arose in modern discussion but is unlikely. He identifies the disputed fragrant cane with sweet flag or sweet calamus, noting Targum Onkelos, the reed-like description, ancient Near Eastern availability, and the plant's known ritual or medicinal uses as better support. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=2072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:32</a> | How do we know the errors in Jephthah's letter are his and not the author's? | The Ammonite king disregards the nonsensical or erroneous letter, which points to Jephthah's flawed argument rather than authorial ignorance. | He points to the king of Ammon's reaction: he disregards the message rather than answering it. Falk says that reaction makes sense if Jephthah's letter is historically and culturally disconnected, almost a non sequitur, so the narrative is showing Jephthah's bad argument rather than the author's confusion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=2505" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:45</a> | How do we know the flaming sword in Genesis 3 is a seraphim? | He connects cherubim and seraphim in sacred-space imagery, with seraphim driving out impurity. | He argues that cherubim and seraphim naturally appear as paired guardians of sacred space: cherubim preserve the holiness inside, while seraphim use flame to drive impurity out. The "flaming sword" functions as that fiery seraphic element, with wording that avoids confusing holy serpents with the fallen serpent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=2770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:10</a> | Can the Eden serpent be both a seraphim or cherubim and a chaos deity? | He says Ezekiel 28 points more toward a fallen cherub than a chaos deity. | He reads Ezekiel 28 as identifying the Eden figure as an anointed covering cherub who abandons his sacred-space function and becomes unclean. He distinguishes that from Old Testament chaos monsters such as Leviathan, Behemoth, Rahab, and Death, so he sides with a fallen cherub reading rather than Walton's chaos-deity framing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=3262" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:22</a> | Was Satan the cherub and the sword angel the seraphim half of a pair? | He says it is possible, but the text does not specify that arrangement. | He calls the proposal a good observation and says it is possible that the paired seraphim held its place while the cherub fell. But he stresses that Genesis only tells us seraphim were posted to protect Eden, so the exact arrangement remains uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=3416" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:56</a> | What is the difference between the Liverpool Egyptology PhD and other Egyptology doctorates? | He answers in terms of language expectations, training profile, and research specialization. | He says Liverpool had an unusually large Egyptology faculty outside Egypt, giving students more instructors, academic life, specialized courses, and archaeology support than smaller programs. He also lists his language background, mostly from master's work: Hebrew, Greek, Egyptian stages, Akkadian, German, French, some Ugaritic and Hittite, Coptic at PhD level, and Japanese as a hobby. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=3875" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:35</a> | What cultural reason stands behind Moses's command in Numbers 31:17-18? | He frames it as vendetta or clan warfare, not hyperbole, aimed at preventing future revenge. | He says this case is not hyperbole but vendetta in a clan-war setting after Midianite betrayal and fragile marriage alliances. In that kind of society, he argues, surviving boys and already-allied women could become future avengers, so the command reflects brutal clan retribution rather than a general rule. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=4265" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:05</a> | Is the man Joseph meets in Genesis 37:15 an angel? | He says there is no textual warrant for that; the figure is likely an ordinary person. | He reads the man as an ordinary person in a populated area near Shechem, perhaps someone in the fields who had seen or heard Joseph's brothers. Since the text gives no strong or weak warrant for angelic identity, he warns against building exegesis on speculation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=4518" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:18</a> | How does the image of God relate to Mesopotamian and Egyptian material? | He points to cult-image texts, Egyptian *tut*, and Hebrew image terms, while noting he has not read Middleton. | He says there are many Egyptian and broader ancient Near Eastern texts about divine images, including Papyrus Harris, temple kiosks, and Egyptian *tut* as "image" in names like Tutankhamen. He also notes Hebrew *selem* and *demut* can denote cultic images, but limits his answer because he has not read Middleton's work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=4794" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:54</a> | Does Genesis compare women to animals or make them less perfect than men? | He says no and treats that reading as a misunderstanding of the creation account. | He rejects the general claim outright and says he would need a specific text to address a specific argument. As phrased, he sees it as one of many unsupported claims people make about Genesis rather than something grounded in the passage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=4895" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:35</a> | Did David or someone else kill Goliath? | He says David killed the original Goliath; 2 Samuel reflects either a textual variant or another Goliath/brother tradition. | He says David killed the original Goliath in 1 Samuel. The later 2 Samuel passage has textual variants, may refer to Goliath's brother, and in any case occurs in a different historical context late in David's life, so he treats another same-named giant as more likely than contradiction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=5304" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:24</a> | How deep or high was the Reed Sea split? | He suggests Palusiac Estuary lakes around 20 to 40 feet deep, enough to drown an army, with wind setdown as plausible. | He places the Reed Sea in the Palusiac Estuary, where lakes averaged roughly 20 to 40 feet deep. That is deep enough to drown weighed-down soldiers and horses, and he says wind setdown studies make a roughly 50 mph wind plausible for moving that depth of water. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=5468" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:08</a> | What changed socially and spiritually for the man healed at Bethesda? | He discusses restoration to community, purity, and covenant life, not just physical healing. | He says Bethesda originally functioned as a ritual-cleansing pool, but in John 5 the man's body is healed while his character does not appear to change. Falk sees some mobility in the text, no clear evidence for the exact sin, and says the man reports Jesus afterward, so he received bodily mercy without evident repentance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=6105" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:45</a> | Were the plagues directed against Egyptian gods or broader Egyptian culture? | He sees them as a cultural challenge to Pharaoh and Egypt's power structures, not a simple one-to-one list of gods. | He rejects the popular one-plague-per-god scheme as attractive but unsupported in the details. He says the plagues challenge Pharaoh, priests, and Egypt's ability to control the elements; even the firstborn judgment is aimed at Egypt's political and inheritance power structure more than temple deities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=6609" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:09</a> | What does he think of Toby Wilkinson's *Rise and Fall* and other Egypt books? | He calls Wilkinson useful and also recommends authors such as Ian Shaw, Mark Lehner, and Aidan Dodson depending on topic. | He calls *Rise and Fall* an okay general book and says recommendations depend on topic. For broad Egyptian history he likes Ian Shaw's Oxford volume, for Egyptian religion he recommends a Wilkinson gods-and-goddesses book, for pyramids Mark Lehner, and for the Amarna period Aidan Dodson. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=6836" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:56</a> | Are there different types of angels? | He says angel is a job description, while cherubim, seraphim, watchers, principalities, and powers describe other functions or classes. | He explains that "angel" means messenger and is more a job description than a species label for every spiritual being. He distinguishes angels from other beings and says terms such as cherubim, seraphim, watchers, principalities, and powers usually refer to different roles or functions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=7088" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:08</a> | What extra-biblical sources help with first-century Jerusalem and Israelite culture? | He names Josephus, Roman reports, early church fathers, Mishnah and Talmud cautiously, and Philo for Alexandrian Judaism. | He recommends Josephus especially for first-century Judean material, while distrusting him for much older history. He also mentions Roman reports, very early church fathers, the Mishnah and Talmud with discernment, Philo for Alexandrian Judaism, and warns against treating late or polemical sources as straightforward history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=7638" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:18</a> | Wisdom of Solomon 2:12-20 sounds messianic; why reject inspiration? | He appeals to inherited Jewish canon boundaries and notes that many useful texts are not canonical. | He says Christians inherit the Old Testament canon rather than creating it, and that earlier Israelite canonical standards centered on prophetic authority, temple deposit, truthfulness, and non-pseudepigraphy. He does not give a specific defect in Wisdom of Solomon without rereading it, but says useful or messianic-sounding content alone does not make a book canonical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=8131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:31</a> | Were disabled Israelites shunned or treated as punished for sin? | Lepers were shunned and some disabilities limited priestly or temple participation, but disability was not generally treated as proof of sin. | He says lepers were shunned because of ritual uncleanness and possible contagion, while disabilities generally were not treated the same way. Some conditions limited temple access or priestly service because ritual worship required bodily intactness, but disability was not normally assumed to be divine punishment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=8467" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:07</a> | Was he called to academia or to YouTube? | He loved academia, but sees YouTube as the open door and current calling with larger reach. | He says he wanted academia, enjoyed teaching, students, committees, and PhD work, but sees YouTube as where God opened the doors. He argues the channel has reached far more teaching hours, denominations, countries, and kinds of viewers than a normal academic post would have allowed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=9600" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:00</a> | What does he think of reading Genesis days through a liturgical calendar? | He says he cannot evaluate the proposal without reading the book. | After a clarification that the proposal relates Genesis 1 to Israelite feast or liturgical days, he still declines to evaluate it because he has not read the book or excerpts. He says such approaches can open the door to speculation and would need a concrete reading before he comments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=9665" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:05</a> | Who was the clumsiest Pharaoh? | He mentions a Middle Kingdom king who dropped a scepter on someone, but cannot recall the name. | He remembers a Middle Kingdom king, not technically a pharaoh by his terminology, who disembarked from a boat and dropped a royal scepter or crook/flail onto a man below. The man panicked because the regalia had divine status, and the king had to reassure him no offense was intended, but Falk cannot recall the ruler's name. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=9834" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:54</a> | Is Mystery History a good ancient-history source? | No; he treats it as conspiracy or Ancient Aliens-style material and avoids it. | He initially says he does not know the channel and usually gets history from books, then reacts to the description as conspiracy or Ancient Aliens-style content. He says that explains why he has not watched it and that he has better things to do than watch that kind of material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=10135" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:55</a> | Did Greco-Roman thought influence Christianity? | Yes, including logos language, Paul's philosophical quotations, and Roman institutions, though specifics matter. | He says influence is uncontroversial because Christianity arose in a Greco-Roman world shaped by Hellenism and Roman occupation. He names logos language in John, Paul's use of Greek philosophers, possible Platonic allusions, Roman military culture, centurions, and tax collection, while stressing that the specific kind of influence matters. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=10361" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:41</a> | How do we know the number 40 is numerological? | Context and repeated use indicate it, though some cases remain ambiguous. | He says context and repeated patterns of forty can show idiom or numerology, but some passages remain judgment calls. In Joshua 14:7, Caleb's age could be literal or idiomatic, and Falk says the text does not make the decision clear. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=10618" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:58</a> | Has a king ever been eaten by a crocodile? | He knows of no record of that. | He says there is no record of an Egyptian king being eaten by a crocodile and considers it highly unlikely. A king would not normally be alone in a vulnerable place, and attendants would have intervened if he fell into dangerous water. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=10733" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:53</a> | What is his advice for a happy and faithful marriage? | He emphasizes Christ-centered commitment, humor, and involvement in each other's life. | He grounds his advice in shared faith in Christ, fighting fairly, saving themselves for marriage, lifelong commitment, humor, and involvement in each other's work and life. Kiara adds that a spouse should be someone you can live with through the worst parts of the vows: poorer, sicker, and worse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=12074" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:14</a> | Is the widow's mite criticizing priests or praising sacrifice? | He reads it in contrast with the scribes, emphasizing costly sacrifice and hypocrisy. | He says the widow's gift is real sacrifice because it hurts and shows devotion, while the surrounding critique exposes scribes who love honor, devour widows' houses, and offer empty prayers. So he keeps both emphases: her costly devotion and the contrast with hypocritical religious leaders. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=12836" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:56</a> | Should we preach more like Jesus? | He says to give law to the proud and grace to the humble, keeping law and gospel rightly balanced. | He says preaching should not imitate Jesus mechanically but should not hold back truth. His guiding principle is law to the proud and grace to the humble, with balance so preaching does not become all condemnation or all comfort. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=13463" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:23</a> | Are there signs of lower-class or vulgar literacy in Middle or Late Egyptian? | He says there are some signs in late Egyptian speech, graffiti, and literate slaves or stewards, though farmers are harder to assess. | He says Late Egyptian appears to originate from lower-class speech, citing tomb scenes where farmers speak Late Egyptian while narrative text remains Middle Egyptian, plus later elite complaints about vulgar speech. He also notes graffiti and literate slaves or stewards, while stressing that farmers left almost no writing and remain hard to assess. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=13928" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:08</a> | How did Israelites safely cross the Sea of Reeds with crocodiles around? | Storm and wind setdown would drive animals to shelter, and any stranded crocodiles could be avoided or repelled. | He says the crossing likely took four to six hours, not twelve, and occurred during strong wind setdown. Nile crocodiles in brackish or fresh water would likely move with the water or shelter during the storm, and any stranded crocodiles would be visible and could be driven off with staffs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/OZSDvdeTC-Q?t=14284" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:58:04</a> | How powerful are donors in academia and seminaries? | Very powerful, especially through funding, chairs, scholarships, and institutional policy pressure. | He says donors are extremely powerful because tuition alone does not cover operations, salaries, scholarships, buildings, or endowed chairs. Large donors can fund research and shape policy, and he gives examples of seminaries and colleges adjusting dress codes, campus rules, or institutional decisions to keep donors satisfied. |
